topic: I bought a used paperback at a bookstore and the margin notes are in my own handwriting from before I was born
title: I Bought a Used Paperback. The Margin Notes Are in My Handwriting. The Book Was Last Read in 1965. | True Scary Story
description_intro: I bought a battered paperback at a used bookstore in Portland last June. The margin notes are in my own handwriting. The book was last checked out of a private collection in nineteen sixty five. I was born in nineteen eighty three. A handwriting analyst in Eugene confirmed the marginalia is mine. The previous owner died of a heart attack in nineteen sixty seven.
description:
I bought a battered paperback at a used bookstore in Portland last June. The margin notes are in my own handwriting. The book was last checked out of a private collection in nineteen sixty five. I was born in nineteen eighty three. A handwriting analyst in Eugene confirmed the marginalia is mine.

The bookstore was a small basement shop off Hawthorne Boulevard called Burnside Used Books. The paperback was a nineteen sixty two edition of a midcentury detective novel. The previous owner's name was stamped inside the cover with a date. He died of a heart attack in nineteen sixty seven and had no children and no living relatives. I tracked his estate through county probate records.

There is a folded letter tucked into the back of the book that I have only shown one person. The letter is addressed to my mother. It is dated nineteen sixty five. It describes a scar on my left elbow that I did not get until I was eight years old. Watch to the end. The bookstore was demolished last year. The book is currently in a fireproof safe in my closet.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #foundtapes
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: I bought a battered paperback at a used bookstore in Portland last June. Nineteen sixty two edition of a midcentury detective novel. Two dollars. The clerk did not bag it. I read the first chapter on the train home. The margins were dense with notes. The handwriting was small, cramped, and slightly slanted to the right. The letters were drawn in a specific way. The lowercase g had a tail that looped twice. The number seven had a crossbar. The capital R was joined to the next letter without lifting the pen. The handwriting was mine. The previous owner had been dead since nineteen sixty seven.

sections.narration: The bookstore was called Burnside Used Books. It sat in a basement off Hawthorne Boulevard. Concrete steps led down to a single glass door. The owner was a man named Ed Markey who had run the shop since nineteen seventy eight. He kept his stock organized by decade. The detective novel was in a wooden crate labeled NINETEEN SIXTIES PAPERBACKS, ONE DOLLAR. Ed charged me two dollars because he said the cover was in better shape than most. He did not write a receipt. He told me the book had come in two weeks earlier from a private estate sale. He did not remember which one.

sections.narration: I have been buying used paperbacks for fifteen years. About six hundred books on the shelves in my apartment. I look for handwritten marginalia first. Notes in the margins tell you who read the book before you. A previous owner's reactions, underlines, doodles. I have a folder of photographs of every interesting marginalia I have found. Most are short. A name. A date. A grocery list copied onto the inside cover. The detective novel was different. Every page had notes. The handwriting was consistent across two hundred and forty pages. The previous owner had read the book carefully and written down their thoughts in a tight cursive that I recognized as my own.
image_prompts: wide interior of a small Portland apartment with floor to ceiling bookshelves lined with paperbacks, dim warm afternoon light through a window, no people visible, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I noticed the underlines first. The previous reader had underlined a sentence on page thirty one about a detective who only worked at night because the daylight made him nauseous. I have a habit of underlining sentences about characters who are uncomfortable in daylight. I have done it since college. Three of the underlines on the first fifty pages matched sentences I had underlined in other books on my shelf. I checked. Same pen pressure. Same single firm line from left to right with a small upward flick at the end. I told myself it was a coincidence. Two people can have a similar underlining style. I kept reading.

close shot of a magnifying glass held over an underlined sentence in a paperback book, dim reading light, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: By chapter four I had stopped pretending. The margin note on page seventy eight read this character is lying about the train. I have written that exact phrase in another book five years ago. The note on page one twelve read the wife is the one who is afraid, not the husband. That is the kind of observation I make. The doodle in the back cover was a small fish drawn in the corner with three dots under it. I have drawn that exact fish on every notebook I have owned since I was nineteen. The misspelling in the margin note on page one ninety three was a word I misspell. The same way. Every time.
image_prompts: close detail of an open paperback margin showing a handwritten note that reads this character is lying about the train, dim reading light, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: Inside the front cover was a small purple ink stamp. The stamp read FROM THE LIBRARY OF CLARENCE T HOLLISTER, MEDFORD OREGON. A date had been written under the stamp in fountain pen. April nineteen sixty five. I drove to Eugene the following Saturday and showed the book to a handwriting analyst named Janet Pell. She compared the margin notes to a letter I had written to my insurance company two months earlier. She studied them for an hour. She told me the writer of the margin notes and the writer of the letter were the same person. She told me there was no chance she was wrong.
image_prompts: close detail of a small purple ink library stamp inside the front cover of a paperback book reading from the library of a name in faded letters, dim ambient light, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I drove to Medford the following weekend. Clarence T Hollister had lived in a small two bedroom bungalow on Maple Avenue from nineteen forty one until his death. He had no children. He had never married. He had no living relatives at the time of his death. His estate had been donated to a local church. The church had sold his belongings at a yard sale in two thousand eighteen. The detective novel had passed through three estate buyers before arriving at Burnside Used Books last May. I obtained a copy of his obituary from the public library in Medford. He died of a heart attack in November nineteen sixty seven. He was fifty eight years old.
image_prompts: wide exterior of a small two bedroom bungalow on a quiet Medford Oregon street with overgrown landscaping, late afternoon light, no people visible, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: Back at home I cataloged the marginalia in a spreadsheet. Two hundred and forty pages. Three hundred and ninety seven separate notes. By the time I reached chapter sixteen I noticed something. Four pages of the book were missing. Pages two oh one through two oh four. The pages had been carefully removed at the spine. The cut was clean and old. The previous reader had written a single line in the bottom margin of page two hundred. The note read I have folded the important pages into the back. I had not opened the back of the book since I bought it. I lifted the rear cover.
image_prompts: close detail of a paperback book opened to a chapter break showing the spine where four pages have been cleanly cut out, dim ambient light, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: Folded into the cardboard of the rear cover were four small pages of yellow stationery. The letter was addressed to my mother by her maiden name. The date written at the top was the eleventh of November nineteen sixty five. My mother was nine years old in nineteen sixty five. The letter was written in the same handwriting as the margin notes. The letter described a child who had not yet been born. It used my full name including the middle name I spell in an unusual way. It mentioned a small crescent shaped scar on my left elbow. I got that scar at age eight in nineteen ninety one when I fell off my bicycle.
image_prompts: close detail of four small folded pages of yellowed stationery tucked into the inside back cover of a paperback book, dim reading light, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: The letter is currently in a fireproof safe in my bedroom closet. The book is on a shelf above the safe. I have not opened either since November. Burnside Used Books closed in December. Ed Markey retired and the building was demolished in February to make room for a parking lot. I drove past the lot last month. It is empty asphalt now. I called my mother on her birthday and asked if she remembered receiving any letters from a man named Clarence Hollister when she was a child. She said she did not know that name. She also said something I have not been able to stop thinking about. She told me he had been her piano teacher.
